food_subgroup: Deep-fried stuffed pastry snack with chicken and potato filling

ontology.foodex2_description: Samosa — deep-fried pastry snack; FoodEx2 code for filled/stuffed fried pastry snack
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated chicken-samosa FoodEx2 code exists; A0CKP (fried stuffed pastry snack) is the closest defensible parent node.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for samosa or equivalent fried stuffed pastry snack at time of processing.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of chicken samosa is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 160 g in total (approximately 80 g per piece), as commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa, chicken — nutrient composition estimated from ICMR-NIN (Indian Food Composition Tables 2017) entry for samosa with meat filling, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for samosa (fried, meat-filled pastry)
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for chicken samosa specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 Table entry for 'Samosa (meat)' and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried meat-filled pastry data. The ICMR-NIN entry is the primary anchor; USDA data used for secondary macro cross-check. Per-100g values reflect a typical deep-fried chicken-and-potato samosa composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of samosa and similar deep-fried wheat-flour pastry snacks with starchy filling — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried wheat-flour pastry with potato filling (GI ~55, low category)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chicken samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: deep-fried wheat-flour pastry with potato-based starchy filling, which is classified as low GI (≤55) in the Sydney GI Database. The chicken component does not materially alter GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 43.2) / 100 ≈ 23.8, where carbohydrate per 160 g serving = 27.0 × 1.6 = 43.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~496 mg per 2-piece serving) from seasoning, pastry, and frying process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit chicken samosa to occasional consumption, as a 2-piece serving contributes approximately 496 mg sodium (~22% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), and should pair with low-sodium accompaniments rather than chutneys high in added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but high glycemic load (~23.8 per 2-piece serving) driven by refined wheat-flour pastry shell and potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume chicken samosa in strict moderation, as its low glycemic index of 55 is offset by a high glycemic load of approximately 23.8 per 2-piece serving; portion control to one piece and pairing with a high-fibre side can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat (~14.2 g per 100 g) and saturated fat (~3.1 g per 100 g) from deep-frying in vegetable oil, plus moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, chicken samosa should be consumed infrequently due to its deep-fried fat content (~22.7 g total fat per 2-piece serving) and moderate saturated fat (~5.0 g per serving), which can contribute to unfavourable lipid profiles when eaten regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken samosa is a deep-fried, triangular or conical pastry of South Asian origin, widely popular in Singapore's Indian restaurant scene. The crisp outer shell is made from unleavened wheat-flour dough (maida), which is rolled thin, filled, folded into a cone or triangle, and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crunchy. The filling typically combines minced or finely diced spiced chicken with mashed potato, green peas, onion, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and green chilli, producing a savoury, aromatic interior that contrasts with the flaky, crunchy pastry exterior. A standard restaurant serving in Singapore is 2 pieces (approximately 160 g total). Nutritionally, a 2-piece serving provides approximately 419 kcal, 13.6 g protein, 22.7 g fat, and 43.2 g carbohydrate. The low glycemic index (~55) is offset by a high glycemic load (~23.8 per serving), both driven by the refined wheat-flour shell and potato filling. Sodium content is moderate (~496 mg per serving), warranting attention for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared with certified halal chicken. Clinically, chicken samosa is an energy-dense snack that should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is a culturally significant snack served as a starter or tea-time snack across North Indian, Pakistani, and Singaporean Indian cuisines, and is commonly accompanied by mint chutney or tamarind sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken samosa?
How much fat is in a chicken samosa?
What is inside a chicken samosa?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for chicken samosa as a named dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 (samosa with meat filling) as the primary anchor and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried meat-filled pastry data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for deep-fried wheat-flour pastry with starchy filling. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact-match data for chicken samosa is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 'Samosa (meat)' entry and cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried meat-filled pastry data; no exact chicken samosa entry exists in approved primary sources. 2. Serving size of 160 g (2 × 80 g pieces) is estimated from typical restaurant portion weights for Indian samosa in Singapore; actual weights may vary by restaurant. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for chicken samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. The input description contains 'green peace' which is a typographic error for 'green peas'; corrected in this record. 8. Halal status is marked true based on the restaurant context (North Indian halal-certified restaurants on field survey data); this should be verified per individual restaurant. 9. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects trace levels from frying oil; not confirmed zero.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from ICMR-NIN 2017 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry. Added all mandatory multilingual names (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Populated all health context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Corrected input typo 'green peace' to 'green peas'.

Iteration 2: Addressed validator feedback. Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' to comply with G5. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (G4). Updated health_context.type2_diabetes key_factor and guidance, and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect low GI but high GL.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
